title: Data
text:
Scraping
To scrape our data for this project, we are using the pybaseball library. This module contains a multitude of all-purpose functions to access the BaseballReference and Baseball Savant datasets. Through the MLB’s Statcast system, this gives us data for every pitch from the 2025 MLB season with 118 variables, including pitcher and batter ID, pitch type, the result of the pitch, larger game and context data, etc.
The pybaseball call we used was: “all_pitches = statcast(‘2025-03-27’, ‘2025-09-28’)”

5 rows × 118 columns



After filtering by pitches that resulted in the end of an at-bat, this gave us 183,092 baseball events.


Data Cleaning
Further data cleaning steps include filtering by only pitches that resulted in a ball in play (i.e. that have a valid xBA value), removing walks and strikeouts, and standardizing hit location coordinates to better visualize certain features.



Standardized Hit Coordinates


We then had to choose a versatile mix of input features to use for our modeling. We wanted to include a mix of features that describe the pitch itself, as well as the resulting hit. The dataset also includes metadata about the game, including player and team IDs, and xBA as the outcome/predictive variable.
Eventually, our final feature set included 122,071 observations with 7 input values as well as some metadata and labeling features.


Final Codebook



Table 1: Codebook











Variable Name
Description
Type
Values / Notes




game_pk
Unique identifier for each game
Categorical
6 digit ID code


batter
Unique identifier for each batter
Categorical
6 digit ID code


pitcher
Unique identifier for each pitcher
Categorical
6 digit ID code


launch_speed
Speed of the ball off of the bat
Numerical
Measured in miles per hour


launch_angle
Vertical angle of the ball off of the bat
Numerical
Measured in degrees; negative = downward, positive = upward


hit_distance
Distance or estimated distance ball traveled in air before hitting the ground
Numerical
Measured in feet; estimated for home runs that landed in the stands


hit_location_x
x coordinate of the location where the ball first landed
Numerical
location; used to estimate the direction that the ball was hit


hit_location_y
y coordinate of the location where the ball first landed
Numerical
location; used to estimate the direction that the ball was hit


pitch_speed
Speed of the pitch at release
Numerical
Measured in mph


release_spin_rate
Rotation of the ball at release
Numerical
Measured in rotations per minute (rpm)


xBA
Expected batting average
Numerical (proportion)
Range 0-1; Statcast metric that measures the probability that a ball with the given launch speed and angle will result in a hit


events
Result of the play
Categorical
single, double, triple, home_run, field_out, force_out, grounded_into_double_play, etc


team
Batting team name
Categorical
3-letter MLB team name abbreviations (e.g., BOS = Boston Red Sox, NYY = New York Yankees)


hit
Records if a hit occurs
Binary
0-1; 0 = out, 1 = hit

title: Methods
text:
To predict hitting success, we implemented and compared two distinct modeling approaches with slightly different objectives. First, we trained a linear regression model to estimate the probability that a given batted ball would result in a hit, which is functionally identical to the concept of Expected Batting Average (xBA). This model outputs a continuous probability score, allowing us to interpret results on a granular scale rather than a strict classification.

# Code for model 1?

In parallel, we developed a logistic regression model to directly predict a binary outcome for each batted ball, where each instance is classified as either a hit (1) or not a hit (0). This formulation allows us to evaluate performance in a more traditional classification framework and compare it directly against real-world hit outcomes.

# Code for model 2?

Because the dataset contained only seven unique variables with meaningful predictive power, we extended both models by incorporating second-degree interaction terms. This allowed us to capture how combinations of variables—rather than just individual features—jointly influence the probability of a hit. By modeling these interactions, we aimed to better reflect the complexity of batted-ball outcomes.
Finally, we evaluated the logistic regression model using a dynamic classification threshold rather than the default cutoff of 0.5. By adjusting this threshold, we were able to analyze how sensitivity and specificity trade off under different decision rules, and how the model’s interpretation of a “predicted hit” changes depending on the chosen cutoff.

# Training block(s)?

title: Intro
text:
Predicting hitting success in baseball is a challenging problem because there is no single, universally accepted measure of a batter’s skill. Even in an era where baseball is one of the most heavily data-driven sports, especially since the introduction of MLB’s Statcast system in 2015, capturing overall hitting ability remains complex. One promising approach is the use of Expected Batting Average (xBA), a metric that estimates the likelihood that a given batted ball will result in a hit based on its characteristics, such as launch angle and exit velocity. xBA is already computed within Statcast using historical outcomes of similar batted balls, though the exact underlying model is not publicly disclosed and is generally understood to involve some type of nearest-neighbor style comparisons.
In this project, our goal is to build a model that predicts the probability of a batted ball becoming a hit using historical data in a way that aligns with or approximates xBA. Additionally, we will build a classification model to predict the binary outcome of each batted ball (hit or out). We then compare these predictions against traditional hitting statistics to better understand player performance and its relationship to overall seasonal success at both the player and team level.
Our motivation for this topic is driven by a personal interest in baseball and its data. The challenge of quantifying and predicting hitting success has practical implications for player evaluation, team strategy, and fan engagement. We hope to contribute to the past and ongoing literature about how best to measure and understand baseball performance.

title: Predicting Baseball Hitting Success through Advanced Statcast Metrics
text: This website documents our final project for M244, using data science techniques to predict the success of a baseball hit based on Statcast data.
